original_abstract: The rapid advancement of neurotechnologies, particularly when integrated with AI-driven brain data analytics, has triggered significant concerns regarding mental privacy, mental integrity, and cognitive liberty. Collectively represented as "neurorights," these concepts have moved to the forefront of ethical, legal, and policy discourse. While several nations are already integrating neurorights into their constitutional frameworks, and international bodies like UNESCO and the Council of Europe are actively drafting governance guidelines, the field remains fragmented. Current debates often suffer from opaque philosophical assumptions, inconsistent ethical frameworks, and conflicting legal interpretations. This multidisciplinary study addresses these ambiguities by establishing the conceptual, ethical, and legal foundations necessary for a unified understanding of neurorights. By proposing a "minimalist" conceptual framework for mental privacy, integrity, and liberty, this work aims to provide a coherent baseline for future scholarly inquiry, legislative action, and global policy development.
humanized_abstract: The speed at which neurotechnologies are evolving, coupled with the advent of brain data analytics enhanced with Artificial Intelligence (AI), has drawn attention to the sensitive issues of mental privacy, mental integrity and cognitive liberty. These issues are collectively known as “neurorights”, and have become increasingly discussed at an ethical, legal and policy level. Several countries have already included reference to neurorights in their Constitutions, while UNESCO and the Council of Europe are drafting guidelines and recommendations aimed at enhancing the governance of neurotechnologies. However, the discussion of neurorights currently remains divided along a multiplicity of opaque philosophical assumptions, divergent ethical stances, and contested interpretations of existing laws and regulations. This multidisciplinary research aims at filling this conceptual gap by establishing key principles in relation to mental privacy, integrity and liberty, thereby paving the way for further scholarly investigation and policy reforms at international, regional, national and subnational levels, while advancing the scholarship of neurorights through the presentation of a minimalist conceptualisation of these rights, good for future research, legislation and policy making.
